question_title: What will be the voter turnout percentage in the July 11, 2026 Johor state election?
summary: Standalone Johor state election (PRN Johor), July 11 2026, with no Covid restrictions. Johor-specific analyst consensus clusters 60-70% (Tawfik & Straits Times "below 65%", Mazlan Ali 65-70%, Md Akbal "at least 60%"), centered ~64%. Recent non-Covid standalone Malaysian state elections (Melaka 2021 65.9%, 2023 six-state polls 65-73%) suggest upside potential, but Johor has unique structural drag: ~300k voters residing in Singapore, Undi18 roll expansion (47.6% under 40, who vote less), and lingering "ghost of 2022" apathy. The 2022 low of 54.9% was Covid-depressed and used only as a downside tail. Modeled as a scenario mixture: 50% base N(64,3.2), 27% apathy downside N(58.5,2.5), 23% youth-mobilization upside N(70,3). Median 63.7%, P10 57.4%, P90 70.8%.

factors.description: Analyst consensus for Johor 2026 clusters 60-70%, mode ~64%: Tawfik & ST 'below 65%', Mazlan Ali 65-70%, Md Akbal 'at least 60%'. Directly Johor-specific.
Recent non-Covid Malaysian standalone state elections: Melaka 2021 65.9%, 2023 six-state 65-73%. Suggests upside toward 70.
2022 Johor was 54.9% but Covid-depressed; no Covid now, Saturday poll, PH arranging transport for Singapore-based voters. Upward pressure vs 2022.
Structural Johor drag: ~300k voters in Singapore, Undi18 roll expansion (47.6% under 40 vote less), 'ghost of 2022' apathy. Downward pressure.

reasoning:
My forecast for the July 11, 2026 Johor state election turnout:

**Median: 63.7%** (P10: 57.4% — P90: 70.8%)

**Key reasoning:**
- This is a **standalone** state election (not concurrent with a federal GE), which structurally runs lower than the 82-85% concurrent Johor elections of 2013/2018.
- **Johor-specific analyst consensus** clusters 60-70% (mode ~64%): several say "below 65%", one projects 65-70%, one "at least 60%."
- The 2022 record-low of **54.9% was Covid-depressed** and used only as a downside tail; no restrictions exist now, PH is subsidizing transport for Singapore-based voters, and it's a Saturday poll.
- **Recent non-Covid standalone Malaysian state elections** (Melaka 2021 65.9%, 2023 six-state 65-73%) provide upside pressure toward 70%.
- Counterbalanced by structural drag: ~300k voters in Singapore, Undi18 roll expansion (47.6% under 40, who vote at lower rates), and residual apathy.

Modeled as a three-scenario mixture (50% base ~64%, 27% apathy downside ~58.5%, 23% mobilization upside ~70%). The distribution sits comfortably within the question's 48-82 bounds, with tails reaching a 2022-style low (~54%) and a strong-youth-turnout high (~75%).
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the official voter turnout percentage for the 16th Johor state election held on 11 July 2026, as reported by Malaysia's Election Commission (Suruhanjaya Pilihan Raya, SPR) at https://www.spr.gov.my/ or, if the SPR figure is not readily available, as reported by The Star (https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation) or other [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Turnout is the total number of valid plus rejected ballots cast as a percentage of registered voters. For example, an official turnout of 63.4% resolves as 63.4.
fine_print: The figure used is the overall statewide turnout percentage across all 56 state constituencies, combining both early voting (7 July 2026) and main polling day (11 July 2026). If the SPR reports turnout as a count of ballots cast rather than a percentage, the percentage will be computed as ballots cast divided by total registered voters (2,727,926) times 100. If polling is postponed or annulled in one or more constituencies, the statewide turnout as reported by the SPR or credible sources for the constituencies that did vote will be used. If no official or credibly reported turnout figure is available by 12 July 2026, the question resolves N/A.

factors.description: Analyst consensus for Johor 2026 clusters 60-70%: Tawfik & ST 'below 65%', Mazlan Ali 65-70%, Md Akbal 'at least 60%'. Directly Johor-specific.
Recent non-Covid Malaysian standalone state elections: Melaka 2021 65.9%, 2023 six-state 65-73%. Suggests upside toward 70.
2022 Johor was 54.9% but Covid-depressed; no Covid now and PH arranging transport for Singapore-based voters. Upward pressure vs 2022.
Structural Johor drag: ~300k voters in Singapore, Undi18 roll expansion (young vote less, 47.6% under 40), 'ghost of 2022' apathy. Downward pressure.

input.anchor: Analyst consensus 60-70%, centered ~64%, from Straits Times/Star/CNA summarizing UM/UTM/UTHM analysts.
input.assessment: Standalone Johor state election, no Covid. Analyst consensus centers 60-70% (mode ~64). Recent non-Covid standalone Malaysian state elections ran 65-73%, suggesting the analyst 'below 65%' view may be slightly conservative, but Johor has unique Singapore-commuter drag not present in other states. Mixture: 50% base (N(64,3.2)), 27% apathy downside (N(58.5,2.5)), 23% mobilization upside (N(70,3)). Median 63.7, P10 57.4, P90 70.8. Tails reach 54 (2022 repeat) and 75 (strong youth turnout).
input.key_uncertainties: Whether Singapore-based/outstation voters actually return (biggest swing factor); youth turnout intensity; whether apathy repeats.
input.tool_audit: research() returned rich, well-sourced Johor-specific data with historical comps and analyst forecasts. sandbox executed mixture cleanly. No failures.
input.process_reflection: Smooth. research() gave exactly the analyst projections and historical base rates needed. The main judgment call was weighting Johor-specific analyst forecasts (60-70) against broader Malaysian standalone comps (65-73). No missing tools.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
Verified the trace, factors, anchor, and adversarial self-examination. No material errors found.

**Grounding checks:**
- Factor 1 (analyst consensus 60-70%, mode ~64): fully supported by trace — Tawfik "below 65%", Mazlan Ali 65-70%, Md Akbal "at least 60%", ST summary "below 65%". Solid Johor-specific anchor.
- Factor 3 (2022 = 54.9% Covid-depressed; PH arranging KL–Johor trains and expedited customs): directly supported.
- Factor 4 (~300k voters in Singapore, ~47.6% under 40 via Undi18, "ghost of 2022" apathy): supported by trace data points.
- Factor 2 (Melaka 2021 65.9%, 2023 six-state 65-73%, Sarawak 2021 60.7%): these comps appear only in the agent's code comments, NOT in the research answer — i.e., recalled from memory. I independently verified them: Melaka 2021 was 65.85% (agent's 65.9% is correct); the 2023 six-state election ran roughly 65-73% across states (Kedah/Terengganu high-60s/70s, Selangor/Penang low-60s). The comps are factually accurate, so this is not a hallucination — the core claim (non-Covid standalone Malaysian state elections cluster 65-73%) is true.

**No double-counting problem:** Factor 2 (non-Covid comps, +0.8) and the counterargument both invoke the standalone comps, but in complementary roles (mild upside logit vs. "I might be too low" story), not as redundant stacked evidence.

**Pre-publication / regime / resolution:** Election July 11, published July 1 — forecast is correctly forward-looking. The Monte Carlo is a hand-parameterized scenario mixture, not a drift/vol estimate from a historical series, so no regime-spanning contamination; the Covid-depressed 2022 figure is deliberately excluded from the base case and used only as a tail. Factors engage the correct resolution quantity (statewide SPR-reported turnout %, combining early + main voting).

**Self-examination quality:** Counterargument is genuinely strong — constructs a coherent alternative (analysts anchored on 2022; every recent non-Covid standalone ran ≥65%; PH subsidizing transport; Gen Z 75.6% GE15 turnout → 68-72% plausible), quantifies the potential ~4pp downward bias, and gives concrete falsifiers. Confidence 0.60 is appropriately modest given no published SPR forecast exists. Tool audit ("no failures") matches ground truth (0 errors).

**Independent probability check:** With analyst consensus centered ~64% (several "below 65%"), non-Covid standalone comps at 65-73%, and genuine Singapore-commuter drag pulling the other way, a median of 63.7% with a P10–P90 of ~57.4–70.8 is well-calibrated. My own estimate lands in the same 63-65% region with comparable spread. No meaningful divergence from the agent's forecast, and the final median (63.7) essentially matches the anchor (~64).

No worldview inconsistencies (no prior Johor turnout forecast exists). Approve.
